questions.text: Roundworms have all of the following characteristics except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A pseudocoelom is a fluid-filled body cavity in some invertebrates, such as roundworms, that is only partially lined by mesoderm. This "false" coelom, which also has an endoderm lining on the inner side, serves as a hydrostatic skeleton and a space for organs.
Roundworms, or nematodes, are bilaterally symmetrical, meaning they can be divided into two mirror-image halves by a single longitudinal plane. This is a fundamental characteristic of the phylum

The mesoderm is the middle of the three primary germ layers in an embryo, developing between the ectoderm and endoderm during gastrulation. It gives rise to a wide range of tissues and organs, including the circulatory system, muscles, bones, the urogenital system, and the dermis of the skin.

The ectoderm is the outermost of the three primary germ layers in an embryo, responsible for developing tissues like the nervous system (brain, spinal cord, nerves), the epidermis (outer layer of skin), hair, nails, and the lens of the eye. It forms during a process called gastrulation and eventually gives rise to the outer covering and sensory organs of an organism

A notochord is a stiff, flexible rod of tissue found in all chordates during their embryonic stage. It provides structural support to the developing embryo and plays a crucial role in development by sending signals to pattern surrounding tissues

questions.explanation: Only members of the phylum Chordata have notochords. Roundworms are in the phylum Nematoda.

questions.text: A characteristic common to all chordates that is lacking in other animal groups is
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The appearance of pharyngeal gill slits
options.explanation: Pharyngeal slits are filter-feeding organs found among deuterostomes. Pharyngeal slits are repeated openings that appear along the pharynx caudal to the mouth. With this position, they allow for the movement of water in the mouth and out the pharyngeal slits.

options.text: The presence of three germ layers
options.explanation: Germ layer, any of three primary cell layers, formed in the earliest stages of embryonic development, consisting of the endoderm (inner layer), the ectoderm (outer layer), and the mesoderm (middle layer).

Vertebrates are defined by the presence of the vertebral column. In vertebrates, the notochord develops into the vertebral column or spine: a series of bony vertebrae each separated by mobile discs. These vertebrae are always found on the dorsal side of the animal.

A true coelom is lined on all sides by mesoderm which gives rise to muscles that surround the gut as well as underlying the body wall. This allows more efficient digestion because food can be pushed through the digestive tract by muscles. True Coelom. Schizocoeloms are found in animals known as PROTOSTOMATES.

options.text: The embryonic appearance of an archenteron
options.explanation: Archenteron refers to the digestive cavity of an embryo. In the early stages of embryonic development, the embryo takes the form of a blastula. The blastula is a ball of cells that consists of an outer, single layer of cells known as the blastoderm and an inner, hollow area known as the blastocoel.

questions.explanation: Pharyngeal gill slits appear only in chordates. All eumetazoa possess three germ layers and develop an archenteron. A true coelom is shared with other phyla, such as Echinodermata and Arthropoda. Not all chordates have vertebrae (only the vertebrates do).

A plant homozygous for red flowers is crossed with a plant homozygous for white flowers. All the resulting plants have red flowers (F1 generation). When the F1 generation crosses with each other, 18 plants are obtained. 12 plants have red flowers and 6 have white flowers (F2 generation). What ratio is expected in the F2 generation and what ratio has been obtained?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The obtained ratio is 12 red to 6 white, simplifying to 2:1. The expected ratio from a Mendelian cross (Rr x Rr) is 3:1 for red to white flowers.
This option confuses the expected and obtained ratios. The Mendelian expectation is 3:1, not 2:1, and the actual result was 2:1, not 3:1.
The expected ratio in a typical Mendelian cross involving a heterozygous pair is 3:1, not 1:1. The obtained ratio of 2:1 also does not match 3:1.
This option incorrectly assumes a 1:1 expected ratio, which is not typical for a heterozygous cross like Rr x Rr, where the expected ratio is 3:1.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the expected ratio in the F2 generation is 3:1, and the obtained ratio is 2:1. In a typical Mendelian monohybrid cross (Rr x Rr), the expected phenotypic ratio is 3:1, with three plants having the dominant phenotype (red flowers) for every one plant with the recessive phenotype (white flowers). The obtained ratio of 2:1 suggests a deviation from the expected, possibly due to a small sample size or experimental error. The other options incorrectly present the expected and obtained ratios.

questions.text: “Law of independent assortment” states:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: That each pair of alleles assort independently of other pairs of alleles during gamete formation
options.explanation: This statement accurately represents the Law of Independent Assortment. It means that genes located on different chromosomes are inherited independently of each other during gamete formation.

options.text: That alleles of each pair of contrasting trait have unequal probability to assort with the alleles of other pair
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. The Law of Independent Assortment states that alleles segregate independently, regardless of the alleles at other gene loci. The probability of assorting is equal for all alleles.

options.text: That the two coexisting alleles for each trait segregate (separate) from each other at meiosis so that each gamete receives only one of the two alleles
options.explanation: This statement describes the Law of Segregation, not the Law of Independent Assortment. The Law of Segregation states that each individual has two alleles for each gene, and these alleles segregate during the formation of gametes.

options.text: That pertain to inheritance of single trait (monohybrid cross)
options.explanation: The Law of Independent Assortment is not specific to a single trait but applies to genes located on different chromosomes. The term "monohybrid cross" is more closely associated with the Law of Segregation.

questions.explanation: Mendel's law of independent assortment states that the alleles of two (or more) different genes get sorted into gametes independently of one another. In other words, the allele a gamete receives for one gene does not influence the allele received for another gene.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the stage of cellular respiration for which oxygen is not essential:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A process in living organisms involving the production of energy, typically with the intake of oxygen and the release of carbon dioxide from the oxidation of complex organic substances is called respiration. Glycolysis which is the first step in all types of respiration is anaerobic and it does not require free oxygen. However, if oxygen is present aerobic respiration will occur and the pathway will continue to the Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation. And if oxygen is not available, then Anaerobic respiration will occur.

During pyruvic oxidation, each pyruvate molecule loses a carbon dioxide (CO2) and is oxidized, producing one molecule of NADH. The remaining two-carbon fragment combines with coenzyme A (CoA) to form acetyl CoA. This process is an integral part of aerobic respiration and takes place in the mitochondrial matrix. It precedes the entry of acetyl CoA into the citric acid cycle, where further oxidation and energy production occur.

The Krebs cycle, also known as the citric acid cycle or tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, is a key component of cellular respiration in aerobic organisms. It takes place in the mitochondrial matrix, and its primary function is to harvest high-energy electrons from acetyl coenzyme A (acetyl CoA), derived from the breakdown of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins.

The electron transport chain is a series of four protein complexes that couple redox reactions, creating an electrochemical gradient that leads to the creation of ATP in a complete system named oxidative phosphorylation. It occurs in mitochondria in cellular respiration.

questions.explanation: The stage of cellular respiration for which oxygen is not essential is glycolysis. Glycolysis is the first step in cellular respiration and takes place in the cytoplasm of the cell. It does not require oxygen and can occur under both aerobic (with oxygen) and anaerobic (without oxygen) conditions. In the absence of oxygen, glycolysis is followed by either fermentation (in anaerobic conditions) or the continuation of the aerobic respiration process (if oxygen is available).

During this process, pyruvate is converted into acetyl-CoA (acetyl coenzyme A). The reaction involves the removal of a carbon dioxide (CO2) molecule as a by-product. This step is known as the pyruvate decarboxylation reaction. The acetyl-CoA then enters the citric acid cycle (also known as the Krebs cycle) within the mitochondrial matrix.

questions.text: Every molecule of NADH, fed into ETC produces:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Each molecule of NADH that is fed into the electron transport chain (ETC) produces approximately 3 ATP molecules. During oxidative phosphorylation, the NADH donates its electron to the ETC, which leads to the generation of a proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane. As the protons flow back through ATP synthase, ATP is synthesized. Each NADH can contribute enough energy to generate approximately 3 ATP molecules through this process.

As the protons flow back through ATP synthase, ATP is synthesized. Each NADH can contribute enough energy to generate approximately 3 ATP molecules through this process.

During oxidative phosphorylation, the NADH donates its electron to the ETC, which leads to the generation of a proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane. As the protons flow back through ATP synthase, ATP is synthesized. Each NADH can contribute enough energy to generate approximately 3 ATP molecules through this process.

This option is incorrect. Each molecule of NADH that is fed into the electron transport chain (ETC) produces approximately 3 ATP molecules. During oxidative phosphorylation, the NADH donates its electron to the ETC, which leads to the generation of a proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane. As the protons flow back through ATP synthase, ATP is synthesized. Each NADH can contribute enough energy to generate approximately 3 ATP molecules through this process.

questions.explanation: Every molecule of NADH (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) that enters the Electron Transport Chain (ETC) during cellular respiration produces approximately 3 molecules of ATP. This occurs through a process known as oxidative phosphorylation. The high-energy electrons carried by NADH are transferred along the electron transport chain, consisting of a series of protein complexes in the inner mitochondrial membrane. As electrons move through this chain, protons (H+) are pumped across the membrane, creating a proton gradient. This proton gradient is then used by the enzyme ATP synthase to generate ATP from adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and inorganic phosphate (Pi). The exact number of ATP molecules generated per NADH can vary slightly, but, on average, it is considered to be around 3 molecules of ATP. The process is more complex for FADH2 (another electron carrier), which typically yields about 2 molecules of ATP. These ATP molecules contribute to the overall energy production in cellular respiration.

questions.text: Final electron acceptor in respiratory chain is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cytochrome A is a component of the electron transport chain but is not the final acceptor of electrons. It plays a role in transferring electrons from cytochrome b to cytochrome a3.

Oxygen is the final acceptor of electrons in the respiratory chain. It accepts electrons at the end of the electron transport chain and serves as the final electron acceptor in the aerobic respiration, forming water in the process.

Cytochrome a3 is an integral part of the cytochrome c oxidase complex, which is the last protein complex in the electron transport chain. It accepts electrons from cytochrome a and transfers them to oxygen for the formation of water.

Cytochrome C is an intermediary carrier protein that shuttles electrons between complex III (cytochrome bc1 complex) and complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase complex) in the electron transport chain. It is not the final acceptor of electrons.

questions.explanation: The final acceptor of electrons in the respiratory chain is oxygen (O2). During oxidative phosphorylation, which is the last stage of cellular respiration, electrons are transferred through a series of protein complexes in the inner mitochondrial membrane (or the plasma membrane in prokaryotes). This process is part of the electron transport chain.

questions.text: The end product of anaerobic respiration in humans and other mammals is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pyruvic acid is not the end product of anerobic respiration in humans and other mammals. It is the end product of glycolysis, which is the initial step of both aerobic and anerobic respiration.
Ethanol is the end product of anaerobic respiration in certain microorganisms such as yeast. However, in humans and other mammals, ethanol is not produced as the end product of anaerobic respiration.

In humans and other mammals, anaerobic respiration leads to the production of lactic acid. When oxygen availability is limited, such as during intense exercise, the cells switch to anerobic metabolism, and glucose is partially broken down through glycolysis to produce lactic acid.
Glucose is the initial substrate in both aerobic and anerobic respiration. It is not the end product of anaerobic respiration but rather serves as the starting molecule for the process.

questions.explanation: The end product of anaerobic respiration in humans and other mammals is lactic acid. In the absence of sufficient oxygen, cells resort to anaerobic respiration to generate energy. Anaerobic respiration involves the incomplete breakdown of glucose and results in the production of lactic acid as a byproduct. The specific process is known as lactic acid fermentation. During this process, pyruvate, which is an intermediate product of glycolysis, is converted into lactic acid. This reaction helps to regenerate NAD+ from NADH, allowing glycolysis to continue in the absence of oxygen.

questions.text: Movement of the hip joint is which type of synovial joint?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gliding joints permit limited movement in two directions and are typically found in the wrists and ankles. They do not provide the extensive range of motion seen in the hip joint.

The hip joint is classified as a ball-and-socket joint, which allows for multi-directional movement and rotation. This joint type enables a wide range of motions, including flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, and both internal and external rotation.
Pivot joints allow for rotational movement around a single axis, such as the joint between the first and second vertebrae in the neck. They do not accommodate the complex movements of the hip joint.

Hinge joints permit movement primarily in one direction, similar to a door. This type of joint is exemplified by the elbow and knee, which contrasts with the multi-directional capabilities of the hip joint.

questions.explanation: The hip joint allows movement in all directions, including rotation. This wide range of motion is characteristic of a ball and socket synovial joint.

questions.text: The type of sclerenchyma cells found in the seed coat is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fibers are long, narrow, and tapering sclerenchyma cells that typically occur in bundles or strands.
Tracheids are a type of xylem cell, not a type of sclerenchyma cell.
Sclereids are present in hard seed coats and fruit walls.
Vessels are not a type of sclerenchyma cell.
The two main types of sclerenchyma cells found in seed coats are sclereids and fibers, which provide the seed with strength, hardness, and protection.

questions.explanation: There are two types of sclerenchyma: (1) fibers, which are long, very narrow cells with sharply tapering end walls; and (2) sclereids, which are isodiametric to irregular or branched in shape. Fibers are found in the pericycle, xylem, and phloem. Sclereids are present in hard seed coats and fruit walls.

options.explanation: While the coccygeal is the right answer, the lumbar vertebrae are not included in the pelvic vertebrae.
While the sacral vertebrae are included in the answer, the lumbar vertebrae are not a part of the pelvic vertebrae.
The vertebrae that are collectively referred to as pelvic vertebrae are the sacral and coccygeal vertebrae.
The thoracic vertebrae are located in the upper and middle regions of the vertebral column, which are not a part of the pelvic vertebrae.

questions.explanation: The single sacrum is formed by the fusion of five sacral vertebrae. Similarly, the coccyx or tail bone results from the fusion of four small coccygeal vertebrae. The pelvic skeleton is formed in the area of the back, by the sacrum and the coccyx, and anteriorly, and to the left and right sides, by a pair of hip bones. The two hip bones connect the spine with the lower limbs.

questions.text: Bones of the skull are joined by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is correct. Most of the bones of the skull are joined by immovable fibrous joints known as sutures. These joints play a crucial role in protecting the brain by providing a rigid framework.
Sliding joints, also known as plane joints, allow bones to glide over each other and provide flexibility. They are not typically found in the skull but in areas like the wrist and ankle.
Pivot joints allow for rotational movement around a single axis and are found in the neck and forearm. The skull does not contain pivot joints.
Hinge joints allow for movement in one plane, similar to the motion of a door. These are found in areas like the elbow and knee, but not in the skull.
Gliding joints involve bones sliding over one another. These are found in the hands and feet, not in the skull.

questions.explanation: Fibrous joints are characterized by being immovable and are primarily found in the skull, where they are known as sutures. These joints provide stability and protection for the brain by holding the skull bones firmly together. Other joint types, such as sliding, pivot, hinge, and gliding joints, allow varying degrees of movement and are not suitable for the protective function required by the skull.

questions.text: The movement of plants in response to touch stimulus is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrotropism is the growth or movement of a plant in response to moisture or water stimuli.
Chemotropism is a plant's growth movement in response to a chemical stimulus.
Geotropism, also known as gravitropism, is the movement or growth of a plant in response to gravity.
Thigmotropism refers to the movement or growth of a plant in response to a touch or contact stimulus.
Phototropism is the orientation or movement of a plant in response to light stimuli.

questions.explanation: Thigmotropism is a directional growth movement that occurs as a mechanosensory response to a touch stimulus. It is typically found in twining plants and tendrils; however, plant biologists have also found thigmotropic responses in flowering plants and fungi. Phototropism is movement in response to light, geotropism is movement due to gravitational forces, and chemotropism is movement in response to chemicals.

questions.text: Pseudopodia are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: False feet developed in some unicellular organisms
options.explanation: Pseudopodia, which means "false feet" in Greek, are dynamic and flexible projections of the cell membrane that extend outward and retract as needed. They play a crucial role in various cellular processes, including cell movement (locomotion) and engulfing particles (phagocytosis). e.g, they're present on amoeba.

options.text: Small hairlike structures present on the unicellular organisms
options.explanation: Small hair like projections are known as cilia (sing: cilium). Their functions include locomotion, sensory perception and communication. e.g, they're present on surface of paramecium.

options.text: Long, tube like structures coming out of the mouth
options.explanation: Tentacles are elongated, flexible structures found in various organisms, particularly in animals such as jellyfish, sea anemones, and certain worms. They extend outwards from the mouth or oral region and serve various purposes depending on the species.

options.text: Suckers which are attached to the walls of intestines
options.explanation: Flatworms, such as tapeworms (Cestodes) and flukes (Trematodes), are parasitic worms that can infect the digestive system of animals, including humans. These organisms possess specialized structures called suckers or suckling discs, which allow them to attach to the walls of the intestines.

questions.explanation: A pseudopod or pseudopodia is a temporary arm-like projection of a eukaryotic cell membrane that emerges in the direction of movement. The hair-like projections on unicellular organisms are called Cilia. The long tube-like structure connecting the mouth and the stomach is called the oesophagus. The head of some parasites contains structures, suckers which enable the parasite to attach to the gut wall. So option A is correct

questions.text: The system involved in preparing our body in a state of stress and emergency:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nervous system does act quickly in emergencies (like sending signals to the adrenal glands), but it cannot maintain the stress response for long. The endocrine system releases stress hormones (like adrenaline and cortisol) to keep the body ready for longer durations. So, the nervous system alone is not enough.

The endocrine system works more slowly, using hormones carried in the blood. But in an emergency, the body needs instant reactions — like a faster heartbeat and quick reflexes — which come from nervous impulses. So, the endocrine system alone is too slow to handle sudden stress.

During stress or emergency situations, both the nervous system (especially the sympathetic division of the autonomic nervous system) and the endocrine system (particularly the adrenal glands) work together to prepare the body for “fight or flight.” The nervous system quickly sends signals to activate organs and muscles. The endocrine system releases hormones like adrenaline (epinephrine) and cortisol to maintain the response.

The exocrine system involves glands that release substances through ducts (like sweat, saliva, and digestive enzymes). These do not play a role in stress or emergency responses. Therefore, this option is irrelevant to the body’s stress reaction.

questions.explanation: During stress or emergency situations, both the nervous system (especially the sympathetic division of the autonomic nervous system) and the endocrine system (particularly the adrenal glands) work together to prepare the body for “fight or flight.” The nervous system quickly sends signals to activate organs and muscles. The endocrine system releases hormones like adrenaline (epinephrine) and cortisol to maintain the response.

questions.text: What part of the brain integrates sensory information such as pain and temperature?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The forebrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for higher-level functions, such as thinking, planning, and decision-making. It includes the thalamus, hypothalamus, amygdala, hippocampus, and cerebral cortex. The part of the brain that integrates sensory information, such as pain and temperature, is the hypothalamus.
The midbrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for connecting the forebrain and hindbrain. It includes the tectum, tegmentum, and substantia nigra.
The hindbrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for basic functions, such as breathing, heart rate, and balance. It includes the medulla oblongata, pons, and cerebellum.

The spinal cord is a long, thin bundle of nerves that runs from the brainstem to the lower back. It is responsible for transmitting sensory information from the body to the brain and for sending motor commands from the brain to the body.

questions.explanation: Option A: Forebrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for higher-level functions, such as thinking, planning, and decision-making. It includes the thalamus, hypothalamus, amygdala, hippocampus, and cerebral cortex. The part of the brain that integrates sensory information, such as pain and temperature, is the thalamus. The thalamus is a relay center for sensory information, meaning that it receives information from sensory receptors throughout the body and then sends it to the appropriate parts of the brain for processing. The thalamus is located in the forebrain, specifically in the diencephalon. Option B: The Midbrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for connecting the forebrain and hindbrain. It includes the tectum, tegmentum, and substantia nigra. Option C: The Hindbrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for basic functions, such as breathing, heart rate, and balance. It includes the medulla oblongata, pons, and cerebellum. Option D: The Spinal cord is a long, thin bundle of nerves that runs from the brainstem to the lower back. It is responsible for transmitting sensory information from the body to the brain and for sending motor commands from the brain to the body.

questions.text: The hormone which controls the uptake of Sodium ions in kidney and its maintenance in blood plasma is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Growth hormone (GH), also called somatotropin or human growth hormone, is a peptide hormone secreted by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. It stimulates the growth of essentially all tissues of the body, including bone.
Aldosterone is a steroid hormone. Its main role is to regulate salt and water in the body, thus having an effect on blood pressure.

Gonadotropins: Luteinizing and Follicle Stimulating Hormones. Luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) are called gonadotropins because they stimulate the gonads - in males, the testes, and in females, the ovaries.
Thyroxine is the main hormone secreted into the bloodstream by the thyroid gland. It plays vital roles in digestion, heart and muscle function, brain development and maintenance of bones.

questions.text: Which of these is not a reflex action?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Secretion of sweat is not a reflex action. It is controlled by the autonomic nervous system. Hence, this option is the correct one.
Salivation is the secretion of saliva in the mouth when it comes in contact with food or smells food. It is a reflex action. Hence, the option is incorrect.
Flexion is the act of withdrawing the stimulated part of the body when the needle pricks and stimulates pain receptors. It is a reflex action. Hence, the option is incorrect.

options.text: Blinking of eyes due to strong light
options.explanation: In strong light, we blink our eyes due to the stimulation of light-sensitive cells. It is a reflex action. Hence, the option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Reflex actions are quick, automatic responses to some stimuli that are not generated by the brain but by the spinal cord.

questions.text: As an essential element in living organisms, _ ion is playing an important role in insulin secretion, the release of neurotransmitters, muscle contraction, and heartbeat regulation.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sodium is crucial for action potentials and fluid balance, but does not directly facilitate insulin secretion or neurotransmitter release.
Potassium is vital for nerve function and muscle contraction, but it does not play a direct role in insulin secretion or neurotransmitter release.
Calcium ions are essential for insulin secretion and neurotransmitter release, as they trigger various cellular processes, including vesicle movement and muscle contraction.
Chloride is important for maintaining fluid balance and electrical neutrality, but is not directly involved in insulin secretion or neurotransmitter release.

questions.explanation: Calcium ions are critical in facilitating the release of neurotransmitters and insulin. They enter cells in response to specific signals and promote vesicular transport to the cell membrane, culminating in exocytosis. Additionally, calcium ions interact with proteins like troponin, playing a pivotal role in muscle contraction. Sodium and potassium are essential for nerve impulse transmission, while chloride maintains fluid and pH balance but does not directly influence insulin secretion or neurotransmitter release.

questions.text: _ is sometimes given by injection as an emergency treatment in cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter at neuromuscular junctions, at synapses in the ganglia of the visceral motor system. It is a major neurotransmitter outside the CNS, i.e., in the PNS.

Dopamine is a chemical released in the brain that makes you feel good. Having the right amount of dopamine is important both for your body and your brain. Dopamine helps nerve cells to send messages to each other.

Serotonin is a chemical that carries messages between nerve cells in the brain and throughout your body. Serotonin plays a key role in such body functions as mood, sleep, digestion, nausea, wound healing, bone health, and blood clotting.
Epinephrine is the primary drug administered during cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) to reverse cardiac arrest. Epinephrine increases arterial blood pressure and coronary perfusion during CPR.

questions.explanation: When CPR does not generate coronary perfusion of more than 15-20 mmHg, return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) rarely or never occurs. After more than a few minutes of cardiac arrest, arterial tone collapses, and epinephrine or another vasoconstrictor is essential for restoration of cardiac activity.

questions.text: Amylase is not produced by the following type(s) of salivary gland:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The parotid glands are primarily responsible for producing a watery type of saliva rich in amylase, an enzyme that initiates the digestion of carbohydrates.
The submandibular glands secrete a mixed type of saliva containing both serous and mucous components, including amylase for carbohydrate digestion.
The sublingual glands primarily secrete mucus rather than serous fluid, and they do not produce amylase. Their secretion is mainly for lubrication rather than digestion.
All the given options are correct since both the parotid and submandibular glands produce amylase, whereas the sublingual glands do not.

questions.explanation: Amylase is not produced by the sublingual salivary glands. These glands are one of the three major pairs of salivary glands, located beneath the tongue. While they play an important role in saliva secretion, their primary function is to produce mucus-rich saliva, which lubricates the oral cavity and aids in swallowing. Unlike the parotid glands, which secrete enzyme-rich (serous) saliva containing amylase for the breakdown of starch, the sublingual glands mainly secrete mucus and do not contribute significantly to enzymatic digestion.

questions.text: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria in root nodules fix nitrogen in the soil air into:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria convert atmospheric nitrogen (N2) into ammonia (NH3), which is usable by plants.
Nitrite is a form of nitrogen that is produced after ammonia is converted by other bacteria in the soil. It is not the direct product of nitrogen fixation
Nitrate is another form of nitrogen, produced from nitrite. It is also usable by plants, but the initial conversion of atmospheric nitrogen is to ammonia.

Amino acids are building blocks of proteins and are not directly produced from nitrogen fixation. While plants use nitrogen to synthesize amino acids, the initial step is the conversion of atmospheric nitrogen to ammonia.

questions.explanation: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria, such as Rhizobium in root nodules of legumes, convert atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) into ammonia (NH₃). Ammonia can then be used by plants to synthesize amino acids and other nitrogen-containing compounds. Nitrite (NO₂⁻) and nitrate (NO₃⁻) are formed later in the nitrogen cycle through nitrification, not directly by nitrogen-fixing bacteria. Amino acids are produced after plants assimilate ammonia into organic compounds.

questions.text: Digestion of which food components starts from the oral cavity?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Protein is a vital nutrient for almost every part of your body. It's digested in the stomach and small intestine. Most of the protein digestion occurs in the small intestine.

Fat digestion begins in the stomach. Some of the byproducts of fat digestion can be directly absorbed in the stomach. When the fat enters the small intestine, the gallbladder and pancreas secrete substances to further break down the fat.

Digestion of carbohydrates (starches into glucose molecules) starts in the mouth but primarily takes place in the small intestine by the action of specific enzymes secreted from the pancreas (e.g., α-amylase and α-glucosidase).

Essentially, all vitamin absorption occurs in the small intestine. Stomach juices, pancreatic secretions, bile from the liver, and the integrity of the wall of the intestine — all have some influence on how much of the vitamin is absorbed.

questions.explanation: Mechanical and chemical digestion begin in the mouth with the chewing of food and the release of saliva, which starts carbohydrate digestion because saliva contains 'amylase', the enzyme that breaks down starch. The epiglottis covers the trachea so the bolus (ball of chewed food) does not go down into the trachea or lungs, but rather into the esophagus.

questions.text: Which of the following nutrients is incorrectly paired with its function in a plant?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Potassium - Formation of the cell wall
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Potassium is involved in regulating the opening and closing of stomata and is crucial for enzyme activation, not cell wall formation.
This is correct. Magnesium is a central component of the chlorophyll molecule, essential for photosynthesis.
This is correct. Nitrogen is a key component of amino acids, the building blocks of proteins.
This is correct. Phosphorus is a crucial part of nucleic acids like DNA and RNA.

questions.explanation: The plant cell wall is made of Cellulose. Potassium creates the cell membrane potential, which allows the cell to generate an action potential. Magnesium in plants is located in the enzymes, in the heart of the chlorophyll molecule. A phosphate backbone is the portion of the DNA double helix that provides structural support to the molecule.

questions.text: A_ is an organism that makes ATP by aerobic respiration if oxygen is present, but is capable of switching to fermentation if oxygen is absent.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: "Cellular anaerobe" is not a recognized term to describe an organism's respiratory capabilities.
"Respiratory anaerobe" is not a standard term used in microbiology to classify organisms based on their oxygen usage.
Obligate anaerobes can only survive in environments devoid of oxygen and cannot switch to aerobic respiration.
Facultative anaerobes can switch between aerobic respiration and fermentation based on the availability of oxygen, allowing them to thrive in diverse environments.

questions.explanation: Facultative anaerobes are versatile organisms that can produce ATP through aerobic respiration when oxygen is available, and switch to fermentation in its absence. This adaptability allows them to survive in varying oxygen conditions. In contrast, obligate anaerobes cannot tolerate oxygen and rely solely on anaerobic processes. The terms 'cellular anaerobe' and 'respiratory anaerobe' are not used in microbiology to classify organisms based on their oxygen requirements.

questions.text: The muscular tube that passes through the pharynx and joins the stomach is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The trachea is a tube that carries air to the lungs. It is about 10 centimeters long in adults. The trachea is made up of cartilage rings, which keep it open. The trachea is located in the neck, and it is connected to the larynx (voice box) at the top and to the bronchi at the bottom.

An NG tube is a feeding tube that is inserted through the nose and into the stomach. NG stands for nasogastric, which means "through the nose and into the stomach." NG tubes are used to feed people who cannot eat or drink on their own. They are also used to administer medication and to remove fluids from the stomach.
A nerve canal is a passageway for nerves. Nerve canals are found in the spine and in other parts of the body. Nerve canals protect the nerves from damage.
The oesophagus is that part of the digestive tube that extends between the pharynx and the stomach. It is about 25 cm long.

questions.explanation: Option A: The trachea is a tube that carries air to the lungs. It is about 10 centimeters long in adults. The trachea is made up of cartilage rings, which keep it open. The trachea is located in the neck, and it is connected to the larynx (voice box) at the top and to the bronchi at the bottom. Option. B: An NG tube is a feeding tube that is inserted through the nose and into the stomach. NG stands for nasogastric, which means "through the nose and into the stomach." NG tubes are used to feed people who cannot eat or drink on their own. They are also used to administer medication and to remove fluids from the stomach. Option C: A nerve canal is a passageway for nerves. Nerve canals are found in the spine and in other parts of the body. Nerve canals protect the nerves from damage. Option D: The esophagus is a muscular tube that passes through the pharynx and joins the stomach. It is about 25 centimeters long in adults. The esophagus is responsible for moving food from the pharynx to the stomach. The esophagus is made up of smooth muscle, which allows it to contract and relax. This contraction and relaxation help to move food down the esophagus.

questions.text: The diagram represents two liquids, separated by a membrane through which osmosis can occurWhat movement of molecules will occur?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Molecules of dissolved substance move from left to right
options.explanation: The membrane is selectively permeable, meaning it allows only certain molecules to pass through. In osmosis, water molecules move across the membrane, but dissolved substances typically do not pass freely.

options.text: Molecules of dissolved substance move from right to left
options.explanation: The movement of dissolved molecules depends on diffusion, which occurs if the membrane allows their passage. Since osmosis is occurring, it suggests that the membrane does not permit the free movement of dissolved substances.

options.text: Overall, water molecules move from left to right
options.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water from a region of lower solute concentration (left side) to a region of higher solute concentration (right side) through a semi-permeable membrane. Since the right side has more dissolved substances, water moves from left to right to balance the concentration.

options.text: Overall, water molecules move from right to left
options.explanation: Water moves toward the side with a higher solute concentration, not away from it. Since the right side has more dissolved substances, water moves toward that side, not in the opposite direction.
The correct process is osmosis, which results in the net movement of water from the left to the right side. Thus, an appropriate answer exists.

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water from a region of lower solute concentration (left side) to a region of higher solute concentration (right side) through a semi-permeable membrane. Since the right side has more dissolved substances, water moves from left to right to balance the concentration.

questions.text: Cardiac output is increased by all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hypoxia, which is a low oxygen level in the tissues, can actually increase cardiac output. The body compensates for reduced oxygen availability by increasing heart rate and stroke volume to deliver more oxygenated blood to the tissues.
During exercise, cardiac output increases to meet the increased demand for oxygen and nutrients by the working muscles. This is achieved by an increase in both heart rate and stroke volume.
During sleep, cardiac output typically decreases compared to wakefulness. The body's metabolic demands are generally lower during sleep, resulting in a decreased need for an elevated cardiac output.

Pregnancy leads to an increase in cardiac output to meet the increased demands of the growing fetus and maternal tissues. Hormonal and physiological changes during pregnancy result in an increase in both heart rate and stroke volume.

questions.explanation: During sleep, cardiac output decreases due to lower metabolic demand, whereas exercise, hypoxia, and pregnancy increase cardiac output.

questions.text: The causes of cyanosis include:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Deficiency of vitamin C causes scurvy, but has no effect on the oxygen level in blood.
Varicella-zoster virus is a herpes virus. It usually targets skin and nerves but not the oxygen level in blood.
Degeneration of the cartilage of joints is usually due to arthritis, and has no effect on oxygen level in blood.

Interventricular spetum is a thick muscular wall which separates the right and left ventricles. A defect in it can cause the blood to flow in between the two ventricles causing mixing of blood. This lowers oxygen saturation and causes cyanosis.

questions.explanation: A ventricular septal defect allows mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, reducing tissue oxygenation and causing cyanosis.

questions.text: Which valve action results from an increase in pressure in the ventricles of the heart?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The closing of all the heart valves
options.explanation: Not all valves close simultaneously. During ventricular contraction, atrioventricular (AV) valves close first, followed by the opening of the semilunar valves.
Semilunar valves close when ventricular pressure decreases, not when it increases.

options.text: The opening of the bicuspid valve
options.explanation: The bicuspid (mitral) valve opens when ventricular pressure decreases, allowing blood to flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle.

options.text: The opening of the semilunar valves
options.explanation: When ventricular pressure rises above the pressure in the aorta and pulmonary artery, the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary) open to allow blood to be ejected into circulation.

options.text: The opening of the tricuspid valve
options.explanation: The tricuspid valve opens when ventricular pressure decreases allowing blood to flow from the right atrium to the right ventricle.

questions.explanation: When ventricular pressure rises above the pressure in the aorta and pulmonary artery, the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary) open to allow blood to enter aorta and pulmonary arteries.

Arterial blood pressure (120/80 mmHg) is much higher than capillary pressure (20–40 mmHg).

questions.text: A person got an infection, he became ill, but then he survived. What do you think of the immunity he would have developed?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Active immunity involves the body's own immune response to an antigen. However, this option is too broad without specifying how the immunity was acquired.

Artificially induced active immunity occurs when immunity is developed through vaccines, which introduce antigens in a controlled manner. This is not the case here, as the person was infected naturally.

Passive immunity originates from externally produced antibodies, such as those administered through antibody treatments or transferred through maternal antibodies. This immunity is not long-lasting and does not involve the person’s immune system actively responding to an infection.

Naturally induced active immunity occurs when the body generates an immune response after being exposed to a pathogen in a natural environment, resulting in the production of memory cells that provide long-term protection.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Naturally induced active immunity'. This type of immunity develops when a person is exposed to a pathogen through natural infection, prompting the immune system to produce antibodies and memory cells specific to that pathogen. This process leads to long-term immunity.Option A, 'Active immunity', is correct in describing the type of immunity, but it lacks specificity regarding how it was acquired.Option B, 'Artificially induced active immunity', involves immunity developed through vaccines, which is not applicable as the person was naturally exposed to the pathogen.Option C, 'Passive immunity', involves receiving antibodies from an external source, providing immediate but short-lived protection, unlike the body's own prolonged immune response.

questions.text: What is the size of parvovirus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 200nm is too large for a parvovirus. Parvoviruses are actually among the smallest DNA viruses.
This option is incorrect. Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter.
Parvoviruses are non-enveloped, icosahedral particles 18 to 26 nm in diameter. Plus and minus DNA strands are packaged into separate virions in approximately equal proportions.
This option is incorrect and is not the size of parvovirus. Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter.

questions.explanation: Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter. They are non-enveloped viruses with an icosahedral capsid and contain a single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) genome. Because of their tiny size and simple structure, parvoviruses rely heavily on the host cell’s machinery for replication. An example is the human parvovirus B19, which causes fifth disease in children.

questions.text: Eukaryotic, multicellular, and consumers belong to _ kingdom.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Kingdom Monera includes unicellular prokaryotes. Eukaryotic, multicellular organisms that act as consumers are placed in the Animalia kingdom.
Kingdom Protista includes organisms that are both unicellular as well as multicellular. Eukaryotic, multicellular organisms that act as consumers are placed in the Animalia kingdom.
Fungi are multicellular, eukaryotic but they are not consumers but rather have an absorptive mode of nutrition.

Eukaryotic, multicellular organisms that act as consumers are placed in the Animalia kingdom. These organisms don’t make their own food; instead, they rely on eating plants, other animals, or organic material to get energy. Their cells have a true nucleus, and their bodies are usually organized into tissues, organs, and organ systems. The Animalia kingdom includes everything from simple sponges all the way to complex animals like mammals.

questions.explanation: Kingdom Monera: It comprises unicellular, prokaryotic organisms, which can be heterotrophic or autotrophic, having no well-defined nucleus and no organelles.Kingdom Protista: Protists are eukaryotic, unicellular organisms They can be autotrophs or heterotrophs.Kingdom Fungi: Fungi can be single-celled or very complex multicellular organisms. All fungi are heterotrophic, which means that they get the energy they need to live from other organisms.They are eukaryotes.Kingdom Animalia: They are eukaryotic, multicellular, and heterotrophic.

questions.text: The numbers of capsomeres found in adenovirus capsid is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Herpesviruses also have an icosahedral capsid, but theirs is made of 162 capsomeres. These capsomeres form a strong, symmetric shell around the viral DNA. Compared to adenoviruses, the herpes capsid is larger and sits within an additional layer called the tegument, followed by the outer envelope.

This option is not correct for an adenovirus. The whole particle of adenovirus is about 70 to 100 nanometers across. The shell itself is built from 252 repeating protein subunits called capsomeres, which fit together like pieces of a geometric puzzle to form the protective coat around the DNA.

The adenovirus particle consists of an icosahedral protein shell surrounding a protein core that contains the linear, double-stranded DNA genome. The shell, which is 70 to 100 nm in diameter, is made up of 252 structural capsomeres.

The shell, which is 70 to 100 nm in diameter, is made up of 252 structural capsomeres. 162 capsomeres are found in herpes virus. So, option C is correct.

questions.text: Nowadays every newborn gets regular shots of vaccine for polio. It contains _ for polio to make a child immune against this disease.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antibiotics are a class of medications used to treat bacterial infections. They work by either killing bacteria or inhibiting their growth and reproduction. Antibiotics are specifically designed to target bacteria and have no effect on viruses.

Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of foreign substances called antigens. Antibodies play a crucial role in the immune response and provide defense against harmful pathogens, such as bacteria, viruses, and other microorganisms.

The vaccine for polio contains antigens, specifically the inactivated or weakened forms of the poliovirus. These antigens stimulate the immune system to produce an immune response, including the production of antibodies, without causing the actual disease.

Antisera is a blood-derived product that contains antibodies specifically targeted against a particular antigen or group of antigens. It is obtained from the blood of animals or humans that have been immunized with the antigen of interest.

questions.explanation: A vaccine elicits an immune response by the host so that the host is better equipped to fight against the invading organism, in any potential future instance, via the production of memory cells. It may do so by containing the antigens of the invading organism (in this case, poliovirus) so that the host’s immune system produces memory cells against the specific organism, thereby conferring active immunity against the disease. Antibiotics (option A) are not present in vaccines; they are drugs used to treat bacterial illnesses. Antibodies (option B) are not present in vaccines; they are proteins, produced by plasma cells, to ward off infection. They are produced by the immune system to fight an active infection, but they are not used in vaccines since they do not confer active immunity as the host does not produce memory cells against the invading organism. Antisera (option D) is a blood serum preparation that contains antibodies against a certain antigen or disease; they are used as a treatment for certain conditions and do not confer active immunity to the individual.

questions.text: When were bacteriophages discovered by Twort?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Frederick W. Twort, a British bacteriologist, first discovered the bacteriophage phenomenon in 1915.He observed a “glassy transformation” in colonies of Staphylococcus and suggested it was caused by a filterable virus-like agent that could destroy bacteria.
The option is not correct. Bacteriophages were discovered by Frederick Twort in 1915, not 1920.
Frederick W. Twort, a British bacteriologist, first discovered the bacteriophage phenomenon in 1915.
Bacteriophages were discovered independently by Frederick W. Twort in Great Britain (1915) and Félix d'Hérelle in France (1917).

questions.explanation: Bacteriophages were first discovered by Frederick W. Twort in 1915. While working with Staphylococcus colonies, he noticed that some of them suddenly became transparent and failed to grow. Twort suggested that this was caused by a “filterable virus” capable of destroying bacteria, although he was uncertain and did not pursue the finding further.

questions.text: Amino acids mainly differ due to the type or nature of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All amino acids share a carboxyl group, which does not vary between different amino acids.
The R-group, or side chain, is what varies between amino acids, giving each one unique properties. This is the key factor in distinguishing different amino acids from each other.
The amino group is a consistent feature in all amino acids and does not account for differences between them.
While some amino acids have a methyl group, it is not a common feature among all amino acids, nor is it the variable element that distinguishes them.

questions.explanation: Amino acids are distinguished by their R-groups or side chains, which vary in structure and properties. For example, glycine has a hydrogen atom as its R-group, while alanine has a methyl group. The carboxyl and amino groups are consistent parts of the amino acid structure. Not every amino acid has a methyl group, making it an incorrect distinguishing feature. Thus, the R-group is the correct answer, as it is the variable component that differentiates amino acids.

questions.text: The _ group of one amino acid may react with the _ group of another releasing a molecule of water to form a dipeptide.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term 'functional' is too vague. The peptide bond formation specifically involves the carboxyl and amino groups.
Amino groups do not react with each other to form a peptide bond. They must react with carboxyl groups.
Carboxyl groups do not react with each other to form a peptide bond. The reaction involves a carboxyl group and an amino group.
The carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another, releasing a water molecule and forming a peptide bond, resulting in a dipeptide.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Carboxyl, Amino. The peptide bond is formed when the carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another amino acid. This reaction results in the release of a water molecule, a process known as a condensation reaction. The other options are incorrect because they do not involve the correct groups for forming a peptide bond. Specifically, two amino groups or two carboxyl groups cannot form a peptide bond with each other.

questions.text: If R-group is a hydrogen atom, the amino acid will be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Alanine's R-group is a methyl group, denoted as CH3, not a single hydrogen atom.
Glycine is the simplest amino acid where the R-group is indeed a hydrogen atom, making it unique among amino acids.
Leucine has a more complex branched R-group, specifically -CH2-CH-(CH3)2, not just a hydrogen atom.
Tyrosine contains an aromatic ring in its R-group, which is far more complex than a single hydrogen atom.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Glycine. It is the simplest amino acid, distinguished by its R-group being a single hydrogen atom. This simplicity makes it unique among amino acids, which typically have more complex R-groups. Alanine, Leucine, and Tyrosine all have more complex R-groups with different chemical structures, confirming that they do not meet the criteria of having a hydrogen atom as the R-group.

questions.text: Peptide bond is a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The C-O bond is characteristic of the carboxyl group but is not the bond that forms between amino acids in peptide bonds. Thus, this option is incorrect.
This is the correct option. A peptide bond is a covalent bond formed between the carbon atom of the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the nitrogen atom of the amino group of another amino acid.
A C-C bond is a characteristic of carbon chains in organic molecules, but it is not the bond formed in peptide bonds between amino acids. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
A C-O-P bond is not related to peptide bonds but is more relevant to phosphodiester linkages, such as those in nucleic acids. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: A peptide bond is a covalent linkage formed between the carbon atom of the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the nitrogen atom of the amino group of another amino acid, resulting in a C-N bond. This bond formation releases a molecule of water, a process known as a dehydration synthesis or condensation reaction. Options C-O, C-C, and C-O-P describe other types of chemical bonds that do not participate in forming peptide bonds.

questions.text: A dipeptide have an/a _ group at one end and a _ group at the other end of molecule.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct! A dipeptide has an amino group at one end (N-terminus) and a carboxyl group at the other end (C-terminus). These groups are crucial for forming peptide bonds between amino acids.
Incorrect. While the amino group is present, the other end of a dipeptide has a carboxyl group, not a generic 'functional' group.
Incorrect. The term 'functional' is too vague for the specific groups involved in dipeptides. The correct groups are amino and carboxyl.
Incorrect. A dipeptide cannot have an amino group at both ends; one end must be a carboxyl group.

questions.explanation: A dipeptide consists of two amino acids joined by a peptide bond. This bond forms between the amino group of one amino acid and the carboxyl group of the other. As a result, a dipeptide has an amino group at one end (N-terminus) and a carboxyl group at the other end (C-terminus). Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they either misidentify or omit the essential carboxyl group, or incorrectly suggest identical groups at both ends.

questions.text: Lamarck's ideas on biological evolution contained correct and incorrect notions. Which of his ideas is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Acquired traits can be passed on to offspring
options.explanation: Lamarck believed that traits acquired during an organism's lifetime could be passed to the next generation, which is not supported by modern genetics. This idea is incorrect as genetic inheritance involves the transmission of genes, not acquired traits.

options.text: Living forms become perfect with time
options.explanation: The notion that evolution leads to perfection misunderstands the process. Evolution results in adaptations to specific environments, not a universal state of perfection, which is not an accurate representation of evolutionary theory.

options.text: Nervous fluids are passed on from generation to generation
options.explanation: Lamarck's idea of 'nervous fluids' facilitating inheritance lacks scientific basis and evidence. It is not part of modern understandings of genetic mechanisms.

options.text: Evolution is related to changes in adaptation to the environment
options.explanation: Lamarck correctly identified that organisms change over time in response to environmental pressures. This concept of adaptation aligns with modern evolutionary biology, although his proposed mechanisms were incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. Lamarck acknowledged that evolution involves adaptive changes to the environment, a concept that remains central in contemporary evolutionary biology. While his mechanisms, such as the inheritance of acquired characteristics, have been debunked, the idea that adaptation drives evolutionary change is valid. Options A, B, and C reflect misconceptions or unsupported aspects of his theory.

questions.text: Primordial soup is a set of hypothetical conditions on ancient Earth first proposed by?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dmitri Ivanovsky is known for his foundational work in virology and the discovery of viruses, specifically in plants. He did not contribute to theories on the origin of life or the primordial soup hypothesis.
Dmitry Anuchin was an anthropologist and geographer, focusing on human cultures and archaeology. His work does not pertain to biochemical origins or the primordial soup theory.
Nikolay Shatsky was a geologist who contributed significantly to geological sciences, but not to theories about life's biochemical origins like the primordial soup hypothesis.

Alexander Oparin, a Soviet biochemist, is renowned for proposing the primordial soup hypothesis in 1924. He theorised that life began through gradual chemical reactions in early Earth's oceans, leading to complex organic molecules and eventually life. His work laid the groundwork for the study of abiogenesis.

questions.explanation: Alexander Oparin is credited with the primordial soup hypothesis, suggesting that life on Earth originated from a series of chemical reactions in a nutrient-rich 'soup' of organic compounds. This idea was groundbreaking in understanding abiogenesis. The other individuals listed — Dmitri Ivanovsky, Dmitry Anuchin, and Nikolay Shatsky — were influential in their respective fields of virology, anthropology, and geology, but did not propose theories on the origin of life or the primordial soup.

questions.text: The wings of a bird and the wings of a beetle are considered --------
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Taxonomy is the science of naming, describing, and classifying organisms based on their characteristics. While the wings of a bird and beetle could be used as taxonomic markers, they are not reliable indicators of evolutionary relationships or shared ancestry between the two groups.

Phylogenetics is the study of the evolutionary relationships among species based on genetic and other evidence. While the wings of a bird and beetle could be used as phylogenetic markers, they are not reliable indicators of evolutionary relationships or shared ancestry between the two groups, since the wings are not homologous structures.

Homologous structures are structures that have a similar underlying structure and evolutionary origin but may have different functions in different species. The wings of a bird and beetle are not homologous structures, since they do not share a common evolutionary origin or underlying structure, even though they have a similar function.

Analogous structures, on the other hand, are structures that have a similar function but do not share a common evolutionary origin. The wings of a bird and beetle are analogous structures, since they have evolved independently in different groups of organisms to serve a similar function, but have different underlying structures and evolutionary origins.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct since bird wings and insect wings are 'analogous' structures as both of these species have wings that they use for flight and yet their wings came from dissimilar ancestral origins (Divergent evolution).

questions.text: Darwin was greatly influenced by --------
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Charles Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection was influenced by Thomas Malthus book "An Essay on the Principle of Population".

Darwin was familiar with the ideas of Jean-Baptiste Lamarck and his theory of inheritance of acquired characteristics, he ultimately rejected Lamarck's mechanism for evolution in favor of natural selection.

options.text: L. Millers evidence for origin of life
options.explanation: The Miller-Urey experiment, which demonstrated the chemical synthesis of organic compounds from inorganic precursors under simulated early Earth conditions, was not conducted until the mid-20th century, well after Darwin's time, and therefore did not influence his theory of evolution.

Gregor Mendel's paper on inheritance, which laid the foundation for the modern science of genetics, was not published until 1866, several years after the publication of Darwin's "On the Origin of Species" in 1859, and therefore had a little direct influence on Darwin's work.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct since Charles Darwin was greatly influenced by the idea of Malthus on population control. The main theme in his idea was that the population will always overpower the food and other resources which will lead to states of hunger, disease, and struggle. Thus, the natural struggle for survival caught Darwin’s attention and he extended the idea of Malthus in his 'theory of evolution by natural selection'.

questions.text: Discontinuous variations in a population were first observed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mendel's experiments on pea plants demonstrated the existence of discrete or discontinuous variation in a population. He observed that the traits he studied, such as flower color or seed shape, appeared in distinct forms and were inherited in predictable patterns. This was in contrast to the prevailing view at the time, which held that traits were inherited through blending of parental characteristics. Mendel's work laid the foundation for the modern understanding of genetics and the study of inheritance.

He rediscovered and independently verified the work of Mendel in a separate model organism. He also discovered cytoplasmic inheritance, an important extension of Mendel's theories, which demonstrated the existence of extra-chromosomal factors on phenotype.

Nils Herman Nilsson-Ehle. , advanced the "multiple factor" theory, or theory of polygenic inheritance, in which a trait is produced from the cumulative effects of more than one gene. Traits that display a continuous distribution, such as height, hair or skin color, are polygenic.

Darwin is most famous for writing a book called 'On the Origin of Species'. It talked about evolution and how species had changed and developed over time. 7. His ideas were not accepted by everyone at first.

questions.explanation: Mendel's concept of the gene was based on the analysis of hereditary variants—organisms that show identifiable and persistent differences for specific characters. He was the first to establish rules for the genetic analysis of such distinct variants.

questions.text: The genotype of blood group 'AB' in humans is _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In the human blood group system, there are four main types: A, B, AB, and O. Each individual inherits two alleles for the ABO blood group, one from each parent. The A and B alleles are codominant, meaning that both are expressed when they are both present in an individual's genotype. The O allele is recessive, so it is only expressed when an individual has two copies of the O allele. The genotype of blood group AB is IAIB, which means that the individual has inherited one A allele and one B allele from their parents. These two alleles are codominant, so both the A and B antigens are expressed on the surface of their red blood cells. Individuals with blood group AB are considered universal recipients because they can receive blood from individuals with any blood group without causing a transfusion reaction. However, they can only donate blood to individuals with blood group AB.
This genotype represents blood group B, as it has the B allele from one parent and an O allele from the other parent.
This genotype represents blood group A, as it has the A allele from both parents.
This genotype represents blood group O, as it has an O allele from both parents.

questions.explanation: ABO blood type in humans is determined by 3 alleles IA, IB (Dominant), and i (Recessive) Genotype IA shows blood group A. Genotype TB shows blood group B. Genotype Io shows blood group O. But, IA & IB are co-dominant alleles means neither of the dominant alleles can mask the expression of other dominant alleles. So, the genotype IA IB shows blood group AB.

questions.text: DNA synthesis takes place in _ phase of the cell cycle.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a resting phase of the cell cycle. Cells in G0 are not dividing, but they are still metabolically active. Some cells, such as nerve cells and muscle cells, may remain in G0 for the rest of their lives.

This is the first growth phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell grows in size and synthesizes proteins and RNA. The cell also checks its DNA for damage and repairs any damage that is found. If the DNA is damaged beyond repair, the cell may enter a programmed cell death (apoptosis) pathway.

This is the second growth phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell prepares to divide by synthesizing proteins and RNA. The cell also checks its DNA for damage one last time before dividing.
This is the synthesis phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell replicates its DNA. The DNA is replicated twice, once for each of the two new cells that will be produced.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct . During the S (Synthesis) phase of the cell cycle, the cell replicates its entire DNA content, creating two identical sister chromatids for each chromosome. This process begins with DNA helicase unwinding the double helix, after which DNA polymerase uses each original strand as a template to build a new, complementary strand, following base-pairing rules (A with T, G with C). The result is two identical DNA molecules, ensuring that each daughter cell receives a complete and accurate set of genetic material after cell division. G0 phase: This phase is a resting phase of the cell cycle. Cells in G0 are not dividing, but they are still metabolically active. G1 phase: This is the first growth phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell grows in size and synthesizes proteins and RNA. G2 phase: This is the second growth phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell prepares to divide by synthesizing proteins and RNA.S phase: This is the synthesis phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell replicates its DNA.

questions.text: During the formation of an ovum non-disjunction of the sex chromosomes occurred, the ovum was then fertilized by a normal Y-bearing sperm cell. Which one of the following shows the sex chromosome complement of the resulting zygote?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The women have only X non disjunction, and that would cause ovum to have XX. Fertilization with a Y-bearing sperm gives a zygote with XXY. This condition is for Turner's syndrome.
The women have only X non disjunction, and that would cause ovum to have XX. Fertilization with a Y-bearing sperm gives a zygote with XXY. This leads to Klinefelter syndrome.

The women have only X non disjunction, and that would cause ovum to have XX. Fertilization with a Y-bearing sperm gives a zygote with XXY.

questions.explanation: The women have only X non disjunction, and that would cause ovum to have XX. Fertilization with a Y-bearing sperm gives a zygote with XXY . It is an example of Klinefelter's syndrome. Klinefelter syndrome is caused by the random occurrence of an extra X chromosome in a male, leading to a total of 47 chromosomes (XXY) instead of the typical 46 (XY). This error in nondisjunction happens during the formation of the parent's egg or sperm cells, resulting in a fertilized egg with an extra X. In rarer cases, some or all cells in the body can have the extra X (mosaic or poly-X conditions), which can lead to milder or more severe symptoms, respectively. The condition is not inherited and is a random genetic event.

questions.text: Such inflorescence in which the main axis is elongated and bears sessile flowers is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A raceme is a type of inflorescence, which is a group or arrangement of flowers on a plant. In a raceme, the flowers are borne on stalks that are attached at equal intervals along a central stem, with the youngest flowers at the top and the oldest at the bottom.

The inflorescence in which the main axis is elongated and bears sessile flowers is called a spike. In a spike, the flowers are arranged in an acropetal manner, which means that the lowermost flowers mature first and the upper ones mature later. The flowers are directly attached to the main axis without any pedicels or stalks.

A cyme is a type of inflorescence, which is a grouping or arrangement of flowers on a plant. In a cyme, the central or main stem ends in a flower, and subsequent flowers arise in a lateral fashion from the stem, with the terminal bud developing into a flower.
A panicle is a type of compound inflorescence, a structure that consists of a main stalk with multiple branches, each of which bears a series of smaller stalks and flowers.

questions.explanation: The inflorescence in which the main axis is elongated and bears sessile flowers is called a spike. In a spike, the flowers are arranged in an acropetal manner, which means that the lowermost flowers mature first and the upper ones mature later. The flowers are directly attached to the main axis without any pedicels or stalks. Examples of plants with spike inflorescence include wheat, rye, and barley.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a type of plant stem?

options.explanation: A corm is a modified underground stem that is part of the plant's storage organ. It is a swollen, solid structure that stores nutrients and energy for the plant.
Nodes are not a type of plant stem but rather a specific part of a stem. In botany, a node is a region on a stem where leaves, buds, or branches arise
A rhizome is a type of underground stem that grows horizontally, often serving as a storage organ for nutrients and a means of vegetative propagation.
A tendril is a specialized structure in some plants that is used for climbing and support.
Tubers are typically enlarged, fleshy structures that develop along the roots or stems of a plant. They are swollen portions that store starches and other nutrients.

questions.explanation: Nodes are not a type of plant stem but rather a specific part of a stem. In botany, a node is a region on a stem where leaves, buds, or branches arise. It is a critical point along the stem where various structures are attached. The space between two adjacent nodes is referred to as an internode. Nodes are the points on a stem where leaves, branches, or buds emerge. They represent the areas of growth and attachment on a stem rather than being a distinct type of stem.

The term "gymnosperm" means "naked seed." Gymnosperms produce seeds that are not enclosed within a fruit. Instead, seeds are typically borne on the surface of cones or similar structures.

questions.text: The bonding pair of electrons are equally shared between the atoms in?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In hydrogen fluoride (HF), the bonding pair of electrons is not equally shared between the atoms. Fluorine is more electronegative than hydrogen, so it attracts the bonding electrons closer to itself, resulting in a polar covalent bond. As a result, HF has a partial positive charge on the hydrogen atom and a partial negative charge on the fluorine atom.

Similar to HF, in hydrogen chloride (HCl), the bonding pair of electrons is not equally shared between the atoms. Chlorine is more electronegative than hydrogen, leading to a polar covalent bond. HCl has a partial positive charge on the hydrogen atom and a partial negative charge on the chlorine atom.

In water (H2O), the bonding pair of electrons is also not equally shared between the atoms. Oxygen is more electronegative than hydrogen, causing the oxygen atom to pull the electrons towards itself, resulting in a polar covalent bond. Water has a partial positive charge on the hydrogen atoms and a partial negative charge on the oxygen atom.

In hydrogen gas (H2), the bonding pair of electrons is equally shared between the two hydrogen atoms. Both hydrogen atoms have the same electronegativity, so they share the electrons equally, forming a nonpolar covalent bond.

questions.explanation: H2 forms a covalent bond with identical atoms, so the electrons in the bond must be shared equally.

questions.text: Chemical equilibrium is not associated with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Chemical equilibrium is a macroscopic property, meaning that it can be observed on a large scale. For example, if you have a closed container with a mixture of reactants and products, you will be able to see that the amount of reactants and products does not change over time, even though the reaction is still occurring.

options.text: At equilibrium, the amount of reactants = the amount of products
options.explanation: At equilibrium, the amount of reactants may not necessarily be equal to the amount of products. However, at equilibrium the rate of forward reaction = rate of reverse reaction.

options.text: It is established in closed system
options.explanation: True. Equilibrium can only be reached in a closed system, so that reactants and products cannot escape, and the reaction can proceed in both directions.
